baseline.notes: Baseline is technically fine but opens too abstractly and never gives the problem a memorable shape.
baseline.output_excerpt: Cache invalidation is the process of updating or removing stale entries from a cache when the source of truth changes. Common strategies include time-based expiration, write-through, cache-aside, and explicit invalidation. Each strategy trades consistency, latency, and implementation complexity differently.

skill.notes: Strong improvement. The skill version gives the topic a concrete storefront/CMS example, explains why naive TTL-only approaches hurt, and stages the solution choices clearly.
skill.output_excerpt:
Imagine you run an online store. A product page is fast because its title, price, and inventory are cached. Then someone in the admin panel changes the price from $39 to $29. If the cache keeps serving the old version, your site is now fast and wrong, which is a bad combo. That tension is the whole cache invalidation problem.

The easiest fix is to let cached data expire after a fixed amount of time. That works, until you notice the awkward window where the source of truth has changed but the cache has not. Now we have a real tradeoff to work through.

baseline.notes: The baseline explains the mechanism correctly, but the reader has to imagine the object graph alone.
baseline.output_excerpt: Reference counting associates a count with each allocated object. Every new reference increments the count and every removed reference decrements it. When the count reaches zero, the object can be reclaimed. This approach is simple to implement but does not handle cycles on its own.

skill.notes: The skill version stages object creation, assignment, unassignment, and cleanup in a visual way. The cycle limitation also lands more clearly because the anchor stays stable.
skill.output_excerpt:
Think of each object as carrying a little sticky note with a number on it. That number tells us how many live references still point at the object. If the number is 3, three different places still care about it. If the number drops to 0, nobody is using it anymore, so we can clean it up.

Start with one object named `UserProfile`. A variable points to it, so the count is 1. Pass that object into a cache, and the count becomes 2. Drop the original variable, and the count falls back to 1. Once the cache lets go too, the count reaches 0 and cleanup can happen.

baseline.notes: Usable, but reads like a standard tutorial. The reader learns steps but not much feel for why the grouping exists.
baseline.output_excerpt: Base64 is an encoding scheme that converts binary data into a restricted text alphabet of 64 characters. It works by taking input bytes in groups of three, turning them into 24 bits, and then splitting those 24 bits into four 6-bit chunks. Each 6-bit chunk maps to one Base64 character.

skill.notes: The skill version gives the reader a stable transformation model and a stronger plain-language bridge before the formal bit math.
skill.output_excerpt:
Base64 is what you reach for when you need to move raw bytes through a system that behaves better with plain text. The bytes are still the real payload. Base64 just repackages them into a safer-looking alphabet.

The easiest way to picture it is this: take 3 bytes, line them up into one 24-bit strip, then cut that strip into 4 equal 6-bit pieces. Those 6-bit pieces are small enough to map cleanly into a 64-character alphabet. Once you see that repacking step, the rest of Base64 feels much less mysterious.

generation_results.title: Checksum vs cryptographic hash

baseline.notes: Baseline differentiates the terms, but the toy implementation never becomes vivid.
baseline.output_excerpt: A checksum is a simple value computed from data to detect accidental changes. A cryptographic hash is a stronger function that aims to make collisions and reversibility infeasible. Checksums are good for catching corruption. Cryptographic hashes are used in security-sensitive contexts.

skill.notes: Good toy-first framing. The only reason visual_thinking is a 1 instead of 2 is that the sample stayed compact.
skill.output_excerpt:
Start with the laziest possible integrity check: add up the byte values in a file and keep the result. That gives you a checksum. It is cheap, and it will catch some accidental damage. It is also easy to fool. Different inputs can land on the same sum without much effort.

A cryptographic hash is trying to solve a much harsher problem. It should still be deterministic and fast, but it should also make collisions hard to find and the original input hard to reconstruct. The toy version teaches the idea. The stronger version is what you trust when the stakes go up.

generation_results.notes: Useful but slightly less visual than the top cases.

generation_results.title: API docs control

baseline.notes: The baseline wins because concise API docs should stay concise.
baseline.output_excerpt:
makeSignedUrl(url, options) -> string
Creates a signed URL with an expiration time and optional response overrides. Throws when the URL is invalid or signing credentials are missing.

skill.notes: The skill makes the output more article-like than the task wants. This is a healthy negative control.
skill.output_excerpt: If you have ever needed to give someone temporary access to a file without exposing your storage credentials, `makeSignedUrl` is the helper that does the heavy lifting. It takes a URL plus signing options and returns a time-limited link.

generation_results.notes: Important negative control. The skill should not dominate terse reference writing.

summary.overall_notes: The smoke test suggests strong value on long-form explainers, especially tradeoff and walkthrough topics. The negative control behaves correctly: the style is a poor fit for terse API reference docs.
